column11: Water Margin focuses primarily on the chivalric exploits of outlaw heroes, while Jin Ping Mei shifts the narrative lens to the interior domestic world of the merchant Xi Men Qing's household.
The dominant event type shifts from chivalric romance and heroic violence toward the novel of manners, foregrounding everyday domestic life, desire, and social maneuvering.

Jin Ping Mei grants Pan Jin Lian greater psychological depth and human complexity; the moral condemnation imposed on her by the source text is suspended, allowing a more ambiguous reading of her character.

The male protagonist archetype is preserved: both leads are powerful and dangerous figures. However, the source of danger shifts from a supernatural origin (vampirism) to a psychological one (childhood trauma and BDSM inclination). The 'darkness' of the archetype is retained, but its explanatory framework transitions from the supernatural to psychological realism.
The heroine's core emotional arc — the desire to redeem a dangerous man through love — is directly inherited from the source text without transformation.
The novels use the same asymmetric power dynamic between the dominant male lead and the submissive female lead
The historical era is entirely replaced (Victorian London → contemporary London), while the geographical setting is preserved.
The source text's format as a short story collection is transformed into a serialized television drama, with individual episodic cases expanded into season-long narrative arcs.
The foundational social relationship between Holmes and Watson — cohabitation and close intellectual friendship — is directly inherited without transformation.
The source text's single first-person focalizer (Watson as narrator) is replaced by a dual-focalizer structure in the television adaptation, granting both Holmes and Watson equal narrative access.
The protagonist's baseline characterization is significantly intensified: the source text's detached, eccentric rationalism is reframed through a modern clinical lens as high-functioning sociopathy.
